## [00:00 - 03:03]

So what you're looking at right now is an example service document for Eight Loops Social. When you get into the academy, we're going to be working through something like this together in a lot more detail to assess what this should be for you, this figure here, and then how to put this together. But I want to introduce to you the concept of a service document, which is essentially a fancy name for a pricing menu. Because it will serve you really well in the early stages of your business, whether you're still freelancing at this moment or trying to work with a production team, whether that's contractors or maybe you even have a team member on board at this stage. So an overview on how this works, you can see here, this is actually a pretty long document that we have here. We essentially have the service that we provide, and then we list out in detail what exactly is included as part of that service. Now an exercise that is really beneficial that I would encourage you to do is to work through your product or your service overviews and what are the outcomes of those services, why are people tapping into them, and then what's included in that. This is also going back a little bit to your messaging, right, if it reminds you a bit of that exercise that you did in there. Well, when you were doing the messaging part and you had all the things that were included, this is basically what you're putting inside of your service document. Now the reason this is really nifty here is twofold. The first part is that it will help you to calculate how much time an activity actually takes. So for example here we have email sequences for webinar funnels. Now this service includes six emails set up on any platform, one revision with tagging and integrations. You might have workflows in there, etc., etc. So as the founder, as the person that runs through the service, what I can do is go, well, how much time does it take to set up six emails, the tagging, set it up on a CRM, and one revision. If you're not sure what that time is, what I 100% recommend that you do is you take the next time you have to run through this process, you actually time yourself properly because what's actually happening is a lot of people really, really miss underestimate or they underestimate, I should say, how much time an activity takes them. And so when they start hitting brick walls or going over scope with clients, a lot of the times it's your own fault. It's because you underpriced yourself because you underestimated how much time an activity should actually take to do it really well.

## [03:03 - 05:59]

So go through it properly and time how much time it actually takes. So for example here we have six and a half hours. So once you know the price, the length that it takes, you write it down here. Now when you're freelancing, a lot of freelancers will charge by the hour. And so you're able to tell people, well, this will take around six to 10 hours and my cost is $60 per hour. But when you start to get to the agency stage, certainly you want to avoid showing this to clients. People are not paying you for the amount of hours that you're putting in there. They're paying you for an outcome and positioning yourself as someone who solves outcomes versus just someone that's selling you their time is really going to help you to scale your business up a lot easier. So once we identify how much time it takes us internally, we essentially just multiply that figure with our rate. Now this, we don't need to go into this in too much detail. Again, this is stuff we talk about inside the academy. While you're in the incubator, normally your rates are going to vary anywhere from sort of $50 right through to usually around $160 per hour. If you're a beginner and you don't have any experience, a lot of people sit a little bit lower than that just to cut their teeth on a few clients before they start to scale their prices up. So essentially what you do is you come in here and you break down whatever services that you're offering. So for example, let's say you're doing website builds. Well, you might have one where it's website build and you list everything out and is that broken down into other things, right? Is it, is there a website speed service, speed test service that you offer and what's included in that, etc., etc. Remember that we're trying to position ourselves within a niche and that is part of that is looking at a competence or a process that we're really strong at. So you actually shouldn't have a really big document like this. Yours should almost be like three or four lines and you might have packages. So you look here at the bottom, we have packages down here. So if people buy multiple services, we calculate everything together and allow the client to save some costs. But really you shouldn't have too many lines. As you start to grow your niches and your service offering, then this document does become a little bit bigger and bigger. So this is your pricing document. There's a template below that you can use. Please make sure that you start on this, especially once you get in the academy. This document, you will see how it's going to allow you to build a lot of efficiencies and how you work with a team and onboard people into your business.
